# Duality AI Falcon Hackathon: Final Presentation Content (Round 3)
*Note to teammates: Copy/paste these sections directly into your 5-slide deck (PowerPoint/Canva). Keep text minimal on the slides, use this as your talking script.*

---

## Slide 1: Synopsis
**Title: Off-Road Autonomy via Semantic Segmentation**

*   **The Mission:** Segment complex synthetic desert environments generated by Duality AI’s Falcon digital twin to enable unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) to navigate safely.
*   **The Challenge:** Real-world off-road data is dangerous and expensive to collect. We are training on 3,174 synthetic images to recognize 10 highly ambiguous terrain classes (Rocks, Grass, Sky, Logs, etc.) at the pixel level.
*   **Our Approach:** We prioritized a hyper-efficient pipeline. Because we were constrained by local compute (Apple Silicon CPU), we engineered a system to compress 50 hours of traditional training into just 11 minutes without sacrificing modern architectural complexity.

*(Visual Idea: Put one of our `Input Image` vs `Overlay` images from the `predictions/comparisons/` folder here)*

---

## Slide 2: Architecture
**Title: Vision Transformers meet Fast Linear Heads**

We leveraged a two-part hybrid architecture designed for speed and generalization:
*   **The Backbone (DINOv2 ViT-S/14):** 
    *   We used Facebook's DINOv2 Vision Transformer (trained on 142M images). 
    *   **Crucial Decision:** We completely froze this backbone. It simply acts as a powerful "feature extractor" to understand the desert structure, preventing overfitting on synthetic data.
*   **The Brain (ConvNeXt Segmentation Head):**
    *   We built a lightweight, 3-layer Convolutional head right on top of DINOv2.
    *   It takes the 384-dimensional spatial tokens from DINOv2 and outputs a 10-channel probability map.
    *   We upsample this map using Bilinear Interpolation to perfectly match the original 960x540 resolution.

*(Visual Idea: You can draw or use a simple block diagram: [Image] -> [DINOv2 Frozen] -> [ConvNeXt Head] -> [Segmentation Mask])*

---

## Slide 3: Problems Faced & Solutions Engineered
**Title: Breaking Through Bottlenecks**

*   **Problem 1: Crippling Hardware Constraints**
    *   *Barrier:* Forward-passing 3,000+ images through a Transformer on a local CPU took ~100 seconds per epoch. Full training would take **50 hours**—far past the deadline.
    *   *Solution:* **Pre-Cached Latent Space Extraction.** We wrote a script to run DINOv2 *once* across the dataset, saving the embedded tensors to disk. We then trained the ConvNeXt head directly on the SSD-cached tensors. 
    *   *Result:* Training time evaporated from 50 hours down to **seconds per epoch**. 
*   **Problem 2: macOS Environment Disconnects**
    *   *Barrier:* PyTorch `hub.load` threw fatal SSL certificate verification errors on the local macOS environment, preventing model downloads.
    *   *Solution:* Manually bypassed Python's default SSL handler and routed secure traffic through local Apple Keychain certificates to force the ViT download.
*   **Problem 3: Extreme Class Imbalance**
    *   *Barrier:* Sky and Landscape make up ~60% of all pixels, while Logs make up <1%. The model legally ignored rare objects.
    *   *Solution:* We established a baseline Cross-Entropy architecture and are implementing weighted frequency loss and morphological post-processing (cleanup) to force the network to care about rare pixels.

---

## Slide 4: Statistics & Results
**Title: Data by the Numbers**

**Dataset Geometry:**
*   **Train Split:** 2,857 images
*   **Validation Split:** 317 images
*   **Resolution:** 960x540 

**Performance Metrics (Baseline):**
*   **Peak Validation mIoU:** 0.3615 
*   **Training Time:** ~31 minutes total (6 mins extraction + 25 mins training)
*   Our model excels at macro-structures but struggles at the micro-level (logs/clutter).

**Strongest Validated Classes (IoU):**
*   Sky: **96.28%**
*   Dry Grass: **50.98%**
*   Landscape: **49.60%**
*   Trees: **46.86%**

*(Visual Idea: Put the `training_curves.png` and `per_class_iou.png` from the `train_stats/` folder on this slide)*
